meta.title: Install TNP -- The Network Protocol
meta.description: Set up TNP to resolve TNP domains on any device. Change your DNS or install the TNP client.
meta.ogDescription: Set up TNP to resolve TNP domains on any device.

title: Set Up TNP
subtitle: Resolve TNP domains on any device. Pick a method below.

dns.intro: Point your device's DNS to the TNP resolver. TNP domains resolve natively, everything else forwards to Cloudflare (1.1.1.1).

android.title: Android
android.note: Requires Android 9+ with Private DNS support (DNS-over-TLS). Alternatively, set your Wi-Fi DNS to {{dnsIp}}.

ios.title: iOS / iPadOS
steps: Tap the <accent>(i)</accent> next to your network
ios.note: Note: this only applies to the current Wi-Fi network. For system-wide DNS, install the TNP client instead.

windows.title: Windows
steps: Click your active connection (Wi-Fi or Ethernet)
Next to DNS server assignment, click <accent>Edit</accent>

macos.title: macOS
steps: Select your active connection (Wi-Fi or Ethernet)

linux.title: Linux
linux.networkManagerDesc: Open Settings > Network > your connection > IPv4 > set DNS to {{dnsIp}}

router.title: Router
router.intro: Setting DNS on your router applies TNP resolution to every device on your network -- phones, laptops, smart TVs, everything. No per-device setup needed.
steps: Open your router admin panel (usually <code1>192.168.1.1</code1> or <code2>192.168.0.1</code2>)
router.note: Works with any router that lets you customize DNS servers. After this, all devices on the network will resolve TNP domains automatically.

client.intro: The client only intercepts TNP domains -- your regular DNS stays unchanged. This is the recommended setup for desktop devices.

macos.title: macOS
macos.description: The client runs a local DNS proxy that intercepts only TNP domains. All other DNS queries go through your normal resolver untouched.
requirements: Admin password (the installer sets up a resolver config)

linux.title: Linux
linux.description: Works with systemd-resolved, NetworkManager, and standalone resolv.conf setups. The installer detects your DNS configuration automatically.

serve.intro: Make any local service accessible over the TNP overlay network. No port forwarding, no public IP, no firewall configuration needed.
serve.heading: Host a Service
serve.step2Desc: This opens a browser window for SSO. Your token is stored locally and reused for subsequent commands.
serve.step3Desc: Replace <code1>example.ox</code1> with your registered domain and <code2>localhost:80</code2> with the local address of your service.
howItWorksList: The client connects to a relay server and registers your domain as a service node.
Incoming requests to your domain are routed through the relay over an encrypted tunnel.
Your local service receives traffic as if it were served locally -- no exposure to the public internet.
The connection is kept alive with periodic heartbeats. If the client disconnects, the domain goes offline.

relay.intro: Relays are the backbone of the TNP overlay network. They route encrypted traffic between clients and service nodes.
whatRelaysDoList: Accept connections from service nodes and hold them open as tunnels.
Route incoming client requests to the correct service node over those tunnels.
Encrypt all traffic end-to-end -- the relay never sees plaintext data.
Report liveness and capacity to the TNP registry for load balancing.
relay.communityRelayDesc: Community relays help grow the network and improve performance for everyone. You need a server with a public IP and a stable connection.
relay.step3Desc: The relay automatically registers itself with the TNP registry on startup. It will appear on the <link>network status page</link> once active.
relay.githubNote: See the <link>GitHub repository</link> for full documentation and configuration options.
